coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of blue light-emitting graphene quantum dots and their application in flexible nonvolatile memory
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Kou, Lijie
Li, Fushan
Chen, Wei
Guo, Tailiang
coredata.dc:description: We presented a facile method to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from double-walled carbon nanotube with blue light emission in a chlorobenzene solution, which enabled the preparation of GQD–polymer hybrid nanocomposite. The wavelength-dependent fluorescent lifetime of the GQDs was investigated by using time-resolved photoluminescence technique. Significantly, nonvolatile rewritable memory effect was observed for the GQD-based nanocomposite, suggesting the promising applications of GQDs in data storage. Moreover, due to the easy solution process, we demonstrated the design and realization of flexible GQD-based memory device. This work may expand the application of GQDs to the portable electronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Blue light
Polymer
Flexible memory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of GQDs by using chlorobenzene
Microstructural properties of GNSs and GQDs
Electrical properties of GQD-based polymer memory

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the preparation of GQDs from DCNTs.
(a) TEM images of the (a) as-prepared GNSs and (b) extracted GQDs. (c) High resolution TEM image showing the crystal structure of an individual GQD.

PL spectra of PMMA, GNS, GQD, and GQD/PMMA hybrid films excited at 260nm. Inset: photograph of the GQDs chlorobenzene solution taken under visible light and 365nm UV light, from left to right, respectively.
(a) Time-resolved PL decay of GQDs at various wavelengths. (b) Lifetime of the GQDs as a function of wavelengths.

(a) Schematic of the flexible PET/ITO/PMMA:GQDs/Al memory device. (b) I–V characteristic for PET/ITO/PMMA:GQDs/Al device. (c) Write–read–erase–read sequence test results for the memory device. (d) I–V characteristics for the flexible memory device under repeated bending with a radius of curvature of 6mm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of blue light-emitting graphene quantum dots and their application in flexible nonvolatile memory

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronic Technology, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We presented a facile method to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from double-walled carbon nanotube with blue light emission in a chlorobenzene solution, which enabled the preparation of GQD–polymer hybrid nanocomposite. The wavelength-dependent fluorescent lifetime of the GQDs was investigated by using time-resolved photoluminescence technique. Significantly, nonvolatile rewritable memory effect was observed for the GQD-based nanocomposite, suggesting the promising applications of GQDs in data storage. Moreover, due to the easy solution process, we demonstrated the design and realization of flexible GQD-based memory device. This work may expand the application of GQDs to the portable electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene comprises one monolayer of carbon atoms packed into a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice [1,2]. Graphene’s excellent mechanical and electrical properties, such as far superior mobility [3], thermal conductivity [4], current-carrying capabilities [5], and room temperature ballistic transport [6], combined with its compatibility with existing planar silicon-based technology, make it an attractive material for electric, optoelectronic, and photonic devices.

Compared to two-dimensional graphene nanosheets (GNSs) and one-dimensional nanoribbons, zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess strong quantum confinement and edge effects when their sizes are down to 10nm, which induce new physical properties [7]. Hence, GQDs have potential application in many fields, such as photovoltaics, bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, and sensors [8–10].

However, the synthesis of GQDs is only a recent effort. For instance, Yan et al. reported the synthesis of large colloidal GQDs with tunable size through solution chemistry based on oxidative condensation reactions [11]. Shen et al. also demonstrated the fabrication of GQDs with frequency upconverted emission by using a hydrazine hydrate reduction of surface-passivated graphene oxide [12]. Even though some works concerning the synthesis of GQDs have been performed, the preparation of uniform-size GQDs remains inchoate. It is important to develop new approaches to synthesize GQDs with uniform size distribution. On the other hand, due to their unique quantum confinement effect, it has been massively reported that quantum dot materials can serve as charge trapping media and find promising applications in nonvolatile memory devices [13–17]. And theoretical and experimental studies have shown that GQDs possess stronger quantum confinement effect than graphene nanoribbons [18]. In comparison with other quantum dot materials, the nontoxicity of GQD makes it a better candidate for large-scale application in memories. However, up to now, the fabrication and characterization of GQD-based memory devices have not been reported yet.

In this paper, as shown in Fig. 1 , we present a facile method to prepare GQDs from double-walled carbon nanotubes (DCNTs) with blue emission in a chlorobenzene solution, which enables the preparation of GQD–polymer hybrid nanocomposite. The wavelength-dependent fluorescent lifetime of the as-prepared GQDs is investigated by using time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) technique. Significantly, for the GQD-based hybrid nanocomposite, nonvolatile rewritable memory effect is observed for the first time, suggesting the promising applications of GQDs in data storage. Moreover, by using the easy solution process, we demonstrate the design and realization of a flexible GQD-based memory device.

Firstly, the DCNTs were chemically cut into GNSs using a solution process. For the synthesis of GNSs, the DCNTs (0.050g, directly purchased from Shen Zhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd.) were added into a mixture of concentrated H2SO4 (10mL) and HNO3 (5mL). The solution was kept for 4h and stirred at 100°C with reflux for 24h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension was under ultrasonication for 4h. Subsequently the mixture was diluted with deionized water (60mL). The pH value was adjusted to 8 with NaOH solution. After further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 2000Da) for 4days, the final product solution was centrifuged for 1.5h in an ultrafiltration centrifugal tube (retained molecular weight: 3000Da, purchased from Shanghai Jinxin Biotechnology Corporation) to enhance the GNSs concentration. It is proposed that the strong acid solution would open the nanotubes, leading to the formation of GNSs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of GQDs by using chlorobenzene
ce:section.ce:para: Extraction of the GQDs from water solution of GNSs is necessary for the preparation of GQD–polymer hybrid nanocomposites. By mixing chlorobenzene with the GNSs solution in proportion of 1:1, followed by violent shaking and stirring, the bottom solution was extracted after 1h standing, which included GQDs with uniform size distribution.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out to investigate the microstructural properties of the GQDs. The specimen was prepared by drop casting the sample dispersion onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying under room temperature. PL spectra of the GQDs were obtained by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600). And the lifetime of the GQDs was measured on a time-correlated single-photon counting set-up (Horiba Jobin Yvon Tempro).

For the fabrication of a flexible GQD–polymer hybrid memory device, an insulating polymer polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) was used as host material for the GQDs. PMMA was dissolved in the chlorobenzene containing the GQDs at a concentration of 10mg/mL. By using spin-coating technique, the PMMA:GQDs hybrid film with a thickness of 60nm was formed on the surface of a flexible polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate coated with a conductive indium–tin-oxide (ITO) film. Finally, to fabricate a sandwiched structure PET/ITO/PMMA:GQDs/Al, a top Al electrode pad array with a thickness of 150nm and a diameter of 0.5mm of each pad was deposited on the hybrid layer by thermal evaporation.

To investigate the electrical properties of the as-fabricated memory device, current–voltage (I–V) measurements of the memory devices were performed at room temperature by using a semiconductor characterization system (Keithley 4200-SCS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural properties of GNSs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of the GNSs derived from DCNTs. The nanosheets have a wide size distribution between several nm to ∼100nm. The high resolution TEM image in Fig. 2b demonstrates the topographic morphology of the GQDs extracted from GNSs. The diameters of GQDs are mainly distributed in the range of 5–9nm with average diameter of 7nm (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c is the representative image of an individual GQD, indicating high crystallinity of the GQD, with a lattice parameter of 0.322nm, which is in good agreement with previous report [19].

Under the illumination of 365nm UV light, the GQDs exhibit bright blue emission, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3 . To further explore the optical properties of the GNSs and GQDs, PL spectra are studied, as shown in Fig. 3. When excited at 260nm, the DCNT-derived GNSs show a broad PL emission peak at 510nm due to the wide size distribution of the GNSs. The PL emission of the GQDs extracted from the GNSs is significantly narrowed and the PL peak shifts to 405nm, which is corresponding to the relatively small size of the GQDs, and is in agreement with previous reports [20]. The blue emission can also be observed as a shoulder peak in the PL spectrum for the GNSs, which confirms that the GQDs originate from the GNSs. Similar to previous reports, the GQDs also exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior [20]. With the excitation wavelength changes from 260nm to 375nm, the PL peak shifts to longer wavelengths with the strongest peak at 542nm. The hybrid film containing GQDs embedded in a PMMA layer also exhibits a PL peak at 405nm, indicating the presence of GQDs in the nanocomposite.

Fig. 4 a presents the temporal evolution of the PL intensities for the GQDs at various wavelengths, measured by using a time-correlated single-photon counting set-up. The sample is excited by 32ps pulses derived from a 250kHz amplified Ti:sapphire laser with a wavelength of 375nm. The decays of the samples are biexponential and could be fitted by using a second-order equation. The lifetimes of the GQDs at various wavelengths are plotted in Fig. 4b. Considering that the decays are biexponential in nature, we estimate the amplitude-weighted average lifetimes [21]. It is interesting that the decay of the GQDs at short wavelengths is slower than that at long wavelengths. This implies that the bigger GQDs have shorter lifetime, indicative of the existence of an additional relaxation channel for the bigger GQDs in the chlorobenzene solvent. Even though the clarification of the wavelength-dependent lifetime behavior needs further experimental investigation, our proposition is that the excited bigger GQDs with smaller bandgap can readily relax to ground state via energy transfer from GQDs to the surrounding chlorobenzene molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical properties of GQD-based polymer memory
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 b shows the I–V curve for the as-fabricated PET/ITO/PMMA:GQDs/Al memory device, in which the bottom ITO electrode is grounded and the bias is applied to the top Ag electrode. The pristine device exhibits a high resistance >107 Ω, and the voltage is firstly swept in a sequence of 0V→−3V→0V. By steady increase of the negative voltages imposed on the device, a pronounced change of resistance from the pristine high resistance state (HRS) to the relative low resistance state (LRS) is observed at about −1.2V. After the transition from HRS to LRS, the device remains in LRS, as shown in the subsequent sweep from −3V to 0V. In the next sweep of 0V→4V→0V, the device starts from the LRS and the I–V characteristic is N-shaped at the positive bias region. There is a local maximum in the current at V max ∼2.6V and the current magnitude is ∼5×10−2 A. When the bias goes beyond V max, the current decreases rapidly, indicating a negative differential resistance (NDR) region [22]. After the NDR region a local current minimum exists. When the voltage sweeps in the forward direction from V max to 4V, the device keeps in HRS, indicating that the device transfer from LRS to HRS (defined as reset process) at the V max bias. In the next sweep from 0 to −3V, a switch in current is also observed at a threshold voltage of about −1.2V, indicating the device transition from HRS to LRS (defined as set process) again. The distinct bistable states in the voltage range from −1.2 to 2.6V allow a voltage (e.g. 1V) to read the HRS and LRS signal of the memory. The maximum current ratio between the LRS and the HRS for the device at 1V bias is about 103, which is large enough to minimize the probability of misreading. Therefore, this device is demonstrated as a flash memory device with the setting voltage of about −1.2V and resetting voltage of about 2.6V.

A “write–read–erase–read” sequence test is carried out to investigate the switching characteristics of the memory. As shown in Fig. 5c, setting voltage pulse of −2V (with pulse duration of 1s) for each cycle is applied to turn the device to the LRS, and resetting voltage pulse of 4V (with pulse duration of 1s) is applied to switch the device to the HRS. Then the “read” voltage pulse of 1V is applied to distinguish the LRS from the HRS. When a setting pulse is applied on the device, a switching from HRS to LRS is triggered with a high current recorded in the following reading period. In contrast, a resetting pulse switches the device back to HRS. As shown in Fig. 5c, the switching process is reproducible from cycle to cycle, which confirms the feasibility of switching the present devices by pulse. Endurance test was performed on the GQD-based memory device (results not shown). The memory margin keeps over 103 during cycling, and the cell does not show obvious degradation after several hundreds sweep cycles. The endurance measurements ensure that the switching between LRS and HRS is highly controllable, reversible, and reproducible. During the sweep cycles no obvious deterioration is observed, indicative of good endurance of the PET/ITO/PMMA:GQDs/Al device. Fig. 5d shows the I–V characteristics of the flexible memory device under bending with a radius of curvature of 6mm. The device exhibited highly repeatable I–V curves during the sequence sweep processes, indicating the excellent operating stability of the flexible memory device under sharp bending conditions.

It should be noted that the device with a structure of PET/ITO/PMMA/Al did not exhibit current hysteresis upon the applied electrical stimulation. Thus, it is reasonably proposed that the switching mechanism is related to charge trapping and detrapping in the GQDs [23]. When the “write” voltage pulse is applied to the memory device, it can be intuitively proposed that the electrons injected from the Al electrode actually encounters the GQDs on its way when traversing the hybrid layer and is, consequently, captured in the GQDs. The PMMA:GQDs hybrid nanocomposite switched to LRS due to the improved electron transport property under the internal electrical field induced by the charged GQDs. When the “erase” voltage pulse is applied, the memory device can return to its original HRS because of the release of the trapped electrons in the GQDs.

In conclusion, GQDs with strong blue emission in a chlorobenzene solution are prepared from DCNTs by using a facile extraction method. The GQDs show wavelength-dependent lifetime due to the energy transfer from GQDs to surrounding chlorobenzene molecules. The flexible memory device based on GQDs embedded in a PMMA layer exhibits nonvolatile, rewritable, and repeatable memory effect under the applied bias voltage. In addition, the device shows excellent operating stability under sharp bending conditions. The electrical switching mechanism is proposed to be related to the charge trapping and detrapping in GQDs. These results indicate that the GQD-based device by using a flexible sheet are easy to fabricate and hold promise for potential applications in next-generation nonvolatile flash memory devices. This work may expand the application of GQDs to the portable electronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (20103514120009), Scientific Research Project for Universities funded by Fujian Education Department (JK2010005) and National High Technology Research and Development Program of China (863 Program, 2012AA030303).
